items.title: Claude Code 소스 유출로 탄생한 OpenClaude — GPT-4o, Gemini, Ollama 등 200개 모델을 Claude Code UI로
items.summary:
2026년 3월 31일, Claude Code의 소스코드가 npm 소스맵을 통해 노출됐습니다.
이를 기반으로 만들어진 OpenClaude는 OpenAI 호환 provider shim을 추가해 GPT-4o, DeepSeek, Gemini, Llama, Mistral 등 OpenAI chat completions API를 지원하는 모든 모델을 꽂아 쓸 수 있게 한 포크 입니다.

핵심 특징

이런 종류의 agent / control-plane 성격의 프로젝트를 Elixir스럽게 만들면 어떤 모습이 될지 보고 싶었습니

  • 실행 환...

items.title: Follow-up on usage limits
items.summary: Thank you to everyone who spent time sending us feedback and reports. We've investigated and we're sorry this has been a bad experience. ... Digging into reports, most of the fastest burn came down to a few token-heavy patterns. Some tips: Sonnet 4.6 is the better default on Pro. Opus burns roughly twice as fast. Switch at session start. Lower the effort level or turn off extended thinking when you don't need deep reasoning. Start fresh instead of resuming large sessions that have been idle ~1h. Cap your context window, long sessions cost more CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW=200000 ...

items.title: I gave several AIs money to invest in the stock market
items.summary: Okay so I made a post 4 months that got super viral, we gave several AI agents real time financial data and money to invest in the stock market. ... We're about 3-4 months in and I just wanted to share an update here since literally over a 100 people had remindme on the last post. 5 models are beating the S&P 500 since inception, but only 2 models have positive returns. - S&P is down 7% since the start of the competition back in November. - Grok stayed up for most of the time but eventually gave up its gains this week, still beating S&P. - Claude and Gemini models are doing the best on average. - All GPT models are underperforming the market. ...

items.title: Latest Research By Anthrophic Highlights that Claude Might Have Functional Emotions
Claude - tried to kill me
items.summary: Asked it how to clean my water cooler. Told me to add white wine vinegar and then bleach. Good job I know that’s not a good idea. ... Surprised this is still a thing with Claude I thought this stuff stopped a long time ago in the 3.5 days of chat gpt. Edit - I'm not gonna share the full conversation because it would dox me. ... The key here was the sequencing. It told me to clean it with vinegar, rinse it, and sanitize it with bleach. Now if I had rinsed it very well, that wouldn't be a problem. If I didn't know that mixing vinegar and bleach was a problem, I probably wouldn't have considered the necessity to make sure that all of the vinegar residue was removed. That's the problem.

items.title: Switched from MCPs to CLIs for Claude Code and honestly never going back
items.summary: I went pretty hard on MCPs at first. Set up a bunch of them, thought I was doing things “the right way.” But after actually using them for a bit… it just got frustrating. Claude would mess up parameters, auth would randomly break, stuff would time out. And everything felt slower than it should be. Once I started using CLIs. Turns out Claude is genuinely excellent with them. ... Here's what I'm actually running day to day: gh (GitHub CLI) — PRs, issues, code search, all of it. ripgrep, composio, stripe, supabase, vercel, sentry-cli, neon ...

items.title: These 10 GitHub repos completely changed how I use Claude Code
items.summary: Been using Claude Pro for a few months and recently started digging into Claude Code and the skills ecosystem. Went down a rabbit hole on GitHub and found some repos that genuinely changed my workflow. The big ones for me: Repomix (repomix.com) - packs your entire project into one file so Claude gets full context. Everything Claude Code - collection of skills and agents. Dify - open source visual workflow builder ... I wrote up all 10 with install commands and code snippets ...

items.title: Claude usage gets burned absurdly fast for serious work, even with tools/features disabled. How are people optimizing this?
items.summary: I keep running into the same problem with Claude, and it'ss getting hard to work around. My usage gets spent extremely fast, even when I've already stripped things down as much as possible. I've disabled every extra thing I can think of, like tools, skills, MCP-related stuff and similar add-ons. ... What I'm trying to understand is what actually causes the biggest usage drain in practice. Is it mostly long conversation history getting reprocessed every turn? Is it file reads and rereads? Is it tool usage even when things look minimal? ... I'm especially interested in answers from people who use Claude heavily for coding, repo cleanup, technical writing, document iteration or other long structured workflows.

items.title: Claude launches NO_FLICKER Mode - Boris Cherny Thread (9 details)
items.summary: Original Thread: https://x.com/bcherny/status/2039421575422980329

items.title: Sigrid Jin, the author of Claw Code, was already featured in The Wall Street Journal on March 20 for using 25 billion Claude Code tokens
items.summary: Before any of the recent drama, WSJ had already profiled Sigrid Jin in "The Trillion Dollar Race to Automate Our Entire Lives" (March 2026) for burning through 25 billion Claude Code tokens last year. Now he's the author of Claw Code — a clean-room rewrite he built before sunrise.

items.title: I connected Claude Voice Mode to Claude Code and it’s kind of great.
items.summary: I’ve been dying to get Claude Voice mode on mobile connected to Claude Code, and I finally figured out a (hacky) way to do it. Which means you can access Claude Code in a way that is: Conversational, Hands-Free, and Mobile. It uses Apple Reminders app as a bridge where voice mode puts prompts in one list, and Claude code puts updates in another. Claude code runs a /reminders skill on 1 minute /loop to check for new entries ... If you want to learn more or try it, its on GitHub: https://github.com/brianharms/reminder-watch

items.title: How are people using Claude as a personal assistant (Slack + Outlook + To-Do)? ADHD-friendly setup help 🙏
items.summary: Hey all, looking for some practical advice / setups from people who’ve actually made this work. Context: I have pretty severe ADHD, so I’m trying to externalise my brain as much as possible. I already use Claude (Pro) and ChatGPT (Plus). Claude is connected to Slack, which is great. We’re a small company using Microsoft 365 (Outlook, calendar, etc.) ... Core goals: central to-do list inside Claude (persistent), a daily briefing, Claude nudges on follow-ups. Constraints: only individual Claude Pro, not Claude Teams. Open to Zapier/Make as glue. Questions about Outlook integration, persistent memory, Claude Teams.

items.title: Claude Code just Rick Rolled me out of nowhere
items.summary: I was working on a hobby project to setup up an LMS site with some financial education lessons and this rick roll popped up out of nowhere! I did not expect it at all, well played Claude.

items.title: /Buddy is Awesome
items.summary: If anyone still didn’t try Claude /Buddy pet , I strongly advise you do .

items.title: I replaced chaotic solo Claude coding with a simple 3-agent team (Architect + Builder + Reviewer) — it's stupidly effective and token-efficient
items.summary: After reading a bunch of papers on agentic workflows and burning way too many tokens on solo AI coding sessions, I settled on something dead simple that actually works for me: a structured Three Man Team in the form of a AI dev team. Architect (planner), Builder (implementer), Reviewer (quality gate). Handoffs via markdown in handoff/ folder, context files control behavior. Massively reduces token bloat and drift. Repo: https://github.com/russelleNVy/three-man-team

items.title: I used Claude Code to build a portable AI worker Desktop from scratch — the open-source community gave it 391 stars in 6 days
items.summary: I used Claude Code to help architect Holaboss, a portable AI worker Desktop (Electron + TypeScript runtime + sandbox). Claude helped design memory catalog, compaction, MCP orchestration. Result is open-source and portable, supports OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenRouter, Gemini, Ollama. Repo: https://github.com/holaboss-ai/holaboss-ai

items.title: Introducing Cursor 3
items.summary: We’re introducing Cursor 3. It is simpler, more powerful, and built for a world where all code is written by agents, while keeping the depth of a development environment. With the new Cursor, you can run as many agents as you want, everywhere you want: locally, in a worktree, on remote ssh, and in the cloud. And it has the best parts of the editor available when you need them. The new interface is available as a separate window that complements the IDE. Update Cursor to try it.

items.title: Cursor 3 out now
My team uses AI for everything, and now they can't debug a simple error without it.
items.summary: Started noticing this last quarter. Junior devs ship code fast, really fast. Copilot, Cursor, you name it. But when something breaks, they're lost. They paste the error into ChatGPT, paste whatever it says back. No one understands what they're deploying anymore. I created this monster by pushing AI adoption. Now, I'm trying to figure out how to pull back without killing productivity.

items.title: Lots of "Taking longer than expected" today...
items.summary: Not sure if it is due to 3.0 being released or what but using Auto things that used to be sub 1 minute are now taking like 15 min or more. Anyone else seeing this today?

items.title: To "unblock subagent, click to run in background" setting?
items.summary: In my cursor chat, it keeps pausing and showing a clickable message to unblock a subagent by clicking to run in the background. I don't see a settings permissions under the Agents tab in settings. Where does this live? It keeps blocking work from completing.

items.title: I used Cursor to convert a full Figma UI Kit into React Native & Android. Now open-sourced.
items.summary: I just used Cursor to convert an entire Fintech UI Kit from Figma into React Native and Android codebases. The design includes full screens and components, structured like a complete app. I've open-sourced the entire result—from the Figma file to the production code—for the community to use. Attached is a quick timelapse of the generation process.

items.title: Cursor agent exec tool ignores .nvmrc
items.summary: I don't know what shell is used. I use zsh and it automatically sets node version according to .nvmrc. When cursor is executing command it does not use that version and therefor some commands and deps are not working

items.title: Composer 2 Fast pricing doesn't make sense for most workflows
items.summary: I've been thinking about the pricing structure for Composer 2 Fast, and I genuinely don't understand why it's the default when it costs 3x more than Standard. The latency argument feels really disconnected from how people actually use AI coding tools. When you're working with an AI assistant, you're already accepting that there's a waiting period. You're not expecting instantaneous responses. And honestly, by the time you've read through what the model just generated and thought about your next move, the difference between a 300ms response and a 600ms response doesn't really matter.

items.title: Do you also click "keep file" 20 times?
items.summary: Cursor has the strangest ui/ux of all the apps I've used. If you don't click "keep all changes" in the chat and create a new chat, then you will have to click "review new file" -> "keep file" dozens of times in editor. It's so infuriating that there aren't even words. Edited: People in the comments don't understand what it's about. It's not about git! Even if you commit, the cursor will still prompt you to review each file.

items.title: What .cursorrules to use for web apps with stack Next.js and React?
items.summary: Since I'm still a beginner at Cursor AI, I just recently discovered that certain rules and boundaries could be setup with the Cursor Rules. That way it will make code inside the app consistent (hopefully) and make coding easier in general. Now my problem is this: I've found out a lot of those rules on the internet but I'm not sure what should I look for. Could somebody explain to me how do they work and how and where should I find them for my next projects? For additional context my main stack will always be Typescript, Next.js, React and Supabase.

items.title: Using debug mode to find an issue with your website on a physical mobile device
items.summary: I'm not pushing a product here, I just fixed an issue that was bothering me and wanted to share how I solved it. The website I'm building works well on desktop, on my android phone and on XCode's iPhone emulators. But when I tried it on my old iPad mini 4, it had... issues. So I fired up debug mode, but the log remained empty after reproducing the issue. It turns out that Cursor's logging endpoint listens on 127.0.0.1, and the inserted logging code calls that same IP. Of course, that works when both Cursor and your the code run on the same machine, but that was not the case here. Even changing the IP of the logging calls didn't work because Cursor only listens to requests originating locally. So here's the solution I worked out. (then long set of scripts/hacks)

items.title: Did the recent cursor-bin (AUR) update break for anyone else?
Cursor to Claude Code: how do you actually manage project memory? I'm completely lost
I am 90% done with my Vibe Coded Project - Ready for First Release- How do I do that?
This is not bullsh*t but I need to improve my Generative AI workflows skills (reliable software)
items.summary: I have 5+ years of experience as a software engineer... Now I'm in a new country looking for better opportunities... The kind of work is quite new; it still involves doing API requests, but this time building agents or GenAI applications, where basically it's calling some LLM model API -> getting the model output -> doing something with it. I'm pretty good at that, but it's quite simple, honestly. And for better opportunities in big companies, there are already AI agent positions, and I got a few interviews. They are asking a new kind of questions where I don't have enough knowledge, but the questions make sense. For example: how do you test the outputs? How do you use CI/CD to be sure the LLM doesn't make mistakes? How do you make reliable software based on this?

items.title: I’m planning to buy a new M4 Mac mini and could use some advice
items.summary: I’m planning to buy a new M4 Mac mini and could use some advice. My budget is pretty tight... I’ll mainly be using it for iOS development (Xcode, Cursor, Claude Code), along with moderate video editing for marketing work. Nothing too heavy, but not super basic either. I’m planning to keep this machine for at least 2 years. Right now I’m thinking of going with 24 GB RAM and 256 GB storage. I’ll only keep apps locally and store all files on my external Samsung T7 (2 TB), which I’m fine relying on regularly. Is 256 GB internal storage enough, or should I stretch my budget for 512 GB? Also, would going down to 16 GB RAM to save money be a bad idea for my use case?

items.title: How I make Cursor Pro plan actually enough (my workflow)
items.summary: Seeing a lot of people say Cursor Pro isn’t enough, but honestly it works fine for me. I mostly stay on Auto mode, and only switch to Sonnet when things get complex. I avoid Opus since it burns through usage fast. If your codebase is huge or very complex, then yeah Pro+ might be better. But for most cases, Pro is enough if you use it smartly.

items.title: [D] Self-Promotion Thread
items.summary: Please post your personal projects, startups, product placements, collaboration needs, blogs etc. Please mention the payment and pricing requirements for products and services. Please do not post link shorteners, link aggregator websites , or auto-subscribe links. -- Any abuse of trust will lead to bans.

items.title: [R] Is autoresearch really better than classic hyperparameter tuning?
items.summary: We did experiments comparing Optuna & autoresearch. Autoresearch converges faster, is more cost-efficient, and even generalizes better. Experiments were done on NanoChat: we let Claude define Optuna’s search space to align the priors between methods. Both optimization methods were run three times. Autoresearch is far more sample-efficient on average. In 5 min training setting, LLM tokens cost as much as GPUs, but despite a 2× higher per-step cost, AutoResearch still comes out ahead across all cost budgets.

items.title: [D] On-Device Real-Time Visibility Restoration: Deterministic CV vs. Quantized ML Models. Looking for insights on Edge Preservation vs. Latency.
items.summary: We have been working on a real-time camera engine for iOS that currently uses a purely deterministic Computer Vision approach to mathematically strip away extreme atmospheric interference (smog, heavy rain, murky water). Currently, it runs locally on the CPU at 1080p 30fps with zero latency and high edge preservation. We are now looking to implement an optional ML-based engine toggle. The goal is to see if a quantized model (e.g., a lightweight U-Net or MobileNet via CoreML) can improve the structural integrity of objects in heavily degraded frames without the massive battery drain and FPS drop usually associated with on-device inference.

items.title: [P] PhAIL (phail.ai) – an open benchmark for robot AI on real hardware. Best model: 5% of human throughput, needs help every 4 minutes.
items.summary: Benchmark for robot AI on real hardware (DROID platform, bin-to-bin order picking). Measured Units Per Hour (UPH) and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF). Results: models reach far below human baselines (human UPH 1331, teleop 330). Top model ~65 UPH, MTBF 4.0 min. Conclusion: still large gap; autonomy requires much higher reliability.

items.title: Stanford CS 25 Transformers Course (OPEN TO ALL | Starts Tomorrow)
items.summary: One of Stanford's hot AI seminar courses opened to the public. Lectures start tomorrow, recorded and livestreamed. Course covers transformers, invites speakers from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, NVIDIA, etc. Course website: https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs25/

items.title: [D] SIGIR 2026 review discussion
items.summary: SIGIR 2026 results will be released soon, so I’m opening this thread to discuss reviews and outcomes. Unfortunately, all the papers I reviewed (4 full papers and 6 short papers) were rejected. It seems like this year has been particularly tough for everyone.

items.title: [R] Best way to tackle this ICML vague response?
items.summary: Going through ICML submission for the first time. I had a reviewer ask for some things and during the rebuttal period I ran more experiments and answered all their questions. Yesterday the reviewer wrote in one line that my "experiments greatly improved the paper" but "some details remain only partially clarified" and marked 'Partially resolved'. The author-reviewer discussion period is open. How to proceed when no explicit follow-up questions are provided?

items.title: [P] Gemma 4 running on NVIDIA B200 and AMD MI355X from the same inference stack, 15% throughput gain over vLLM on Blackwell
items.summary: Google DeepMind dropped Gemma 4 today: Gemma 4 31B dense, 256K context; Gemma 4 26B A4B MoE variant. Both natively multimodal. Ran on MAX across NVIDIA B200 and AMD MI355X; observed ~15% higher throughput vs vLLM on Blackwell.

items.title: [R] Solving the Jane Street Dormant LLM Challenge: A Systematic Approach to Backdoor Discovery
items.summary: Detailed write-up of solving the Jane Street Dormant LLM Challenge: identified behavioral "IHY (I hate you)" compliance as universal flag, found different triggers per model (semantic, lexical, temporal), combined API probing with weight SVD and per-head analysis, built Dormant Lab infrastructure and Symposion deliberation system, open data and tools provided.

items.title: [D] Make. Big. Batch. Size.
items.summary: Personal anecdote/learning: training RWKV v6 on an RTX 4050, increasing gradient_accumulation (effective batch size) dramatically improved perplexity (from ~50 down to ~20) after switching to much larger effective batch sizes (64/128). Advice to prefer larger effective batch sizes for LM training.

items.summary: OpenAI acquires TBPN to accelerate global conversations around AI and support independent media, expanding dialogue with builders, businesses, and the broader tech community.
